name: City God
description: The City God, or Chenghuangshen (城隍神), is a tutelary deity in Chinese mythology who protects the people and the city within the walls of a specific city. Each city typically has its own City God, and their ranks and powers are believed to mirror the bureaucratic system of imperial China, acting as spiritual magistrates. They are often seen as deceased officials or prominent local figures who were virtuous in life and appointed to their divine positions posthumously by the Jade Emperor or other high-ranking deities. City Gods are responsible for maintaining order, ensuring prosperity, and protecting the city from calamities. They oversee the souls of the deceased within their jurisdiction, judging their deeds and determining their fate in the afterlife, similar to a judge in the underworld. Festivals and temples are dedicated to them, where people offer prayers for good fortune, protection, and justice. Their power extends to influencing the weather, ensuring good harvests, and resolving disputes. The City God's role is crucial in maintaining the balance between the human and spiritual realms, ensuring the well-being of the city and its inhabitants. The identity of the City God can vary from city to city, reflecting local history and beliefs, but their core function remains the same: to safeguard and govern the spiritual and earthly affairs of their city.
subtitle: Guardian of the City and its People

keyMyths.title: The Righteous Official
keyMyths.description: A virtuous official is appointed City God after death for their just rule and protection of the people. They continue to serve the community in the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: Protection from Calamity
keyMyths.description: The City God warns the city of an impending disaster, allowing the people to prepare and avoid significant harm. This highlights their protective role.

keyMyths.title: Judgment of Souls
keyMyths.description: The City God oversees the judgment of souls, ensuring that justice is served in the afterlife. This reflects their administrative duties.

sources.author: Stephen Feuchtwang
sources.work: Popular Religion in China: The Imperial Metaphor
sources.description: Discusses the bureaucratic structure of Chinese folk religion and the role of City Gods.
sources.text: Discusses the bureaucratic structure of Chinese folk religion and the role of City Gods.
